featured.title: KaDeWe Berlin: A Visitor's Guide to the Grand Store and Its Famous Food Hall
featured.label: Newest shopping guide
featured.summary: KaDeWe is Berlin's grand old department store, and its sixth-floor food hall is the real reason to go. Here is what it is, the free food hall and rooftop, opening hours and Sundays, how to get there, and whether it is worth your time.
featured.alt: The KaDeWe department store building with its red roofline on Tauentzienstraße in west Berlin

miniPosts.title: Kreuzberg Berlin: A Local Guide to the District's Two Very Different Halves (2026)
miniPosts.summary: Kreuzberg is really two districts sharing one name: loud, Turkish, late-night SO36 in the east, and leafy, café-slow Bergmannkiez in the west. Here is what to see and eat in each half, whether it is safe, and which side fits your day.
miniPosts.alt: The Oberbaumbrücke brick bridge over the Spree connecting Kreuzberg and Friedrichshain in Berlin

miniPosts.title: Where Was Hitler's Bunker in Berlin? What You'll Actually Find Today
miniPosts.summary: The site of Hitler's bunker in Berlin is one of the city's most searched-for spots, and there is almost nothing to see. Here is where the Fuhrerbunker really was, what you will and will not find today, why Berlin left it unmarked, and how to visit respectfully.
miniPosts.alt: The site of Hitler's bunker in Berlin today, an ordinary car park with a small information board and apartment blocks behind it

miniPosts.title: How to Pronounce Berlin Station Names: A Simple Guide for Tourists (2026)
miniPosts.summary: German station names can look like someone sat on a keyboard, but they are more predictable than they seem. Here is how to pronounce Berlin station names, built around the stops you will really use, with plain respellings and the sounds that catch visitors out.
miniPosts.alt: Berlin U-Bahn enamel station sign reading Schönhauser Allee, one of the names this guide helps you say

miniPosts.title: Tiergarten Berlin: What's Actually Worth Seeing in the City's Biggest Park
miniPosts.summary: Tiergarten is 210 hectares of woodland, lakes and formal garden sitting right between the Brandenburg Gate and Zoologischer Garten station, open around the clock and free to walk into. Here is what's actually worth seeing, the WWII story behind why the trees are so young, and how much time to give it.
miniPosts.alt: A cyclist crossing an ornamental stone bridge over a calm waterway in Berlin's Tiergarten, framed by spring blossoms

posts.title: Currency Exchange in Berlin: Where to Change Money (and Where Not To) in 2026
posts.summary: Most visitors do not need to exchange cash in Berlin at all. A local guide to getting euros without the hidden margin: bank ATMs, the airport-desk trap, the dynamic-conversion question, and how much cash to actually carry.
posts.alt: Close-up of euro banknotes, a fifty and a twenty, the cash most visitors think they need to exchange before arriving in Berlin

posts.title: Where to Watch the 2026 World Cup in Berlin (No, There's No Brandenburg Gate Fan Mile This Year)
posts.summary: The 2026 World Cup is in North America, and Berlin has no Brandenburg Gate fan mile this year. Here is where to actually watch: free screens, beer gardens and bars, with every kick-off in Berlin time.
posts.alt: A summer evening football public viewing in Berlin with a relaxed crowd, outdoor screen and TV Tower skyline

posts.title: Berlin Observation Decks: Which High View Is Actually Worth Paying For in 2026
posts.summary: Berlin has five public high viewpoints, from the free Reichstag dome to the 203 m TV Tower deck, and they are not equal on price, height or booking. Here is what each one costs in 2026, how you get up, and which Berlin observation deck I would actually send you to.
posts.alt: Warm evening view over central Berlin with the TV Tower rising above the rooftops and the Spree river
